name: fixed-sleep-vs-poll-loop-after-timing-change
tags: deploy.sh, fixed sleep, poll loop, health check, startup time, timing assumption
body:
Any fixed `sleep N` waiting for a process to become ready is a timing assumption that silently depends on startup staying fast — it isn't robust, it's just been lucky so far. Adding literally any new startup-time work (even something as small as a 2.4s checkpoint DB compaction) can push real startup time past the sleep, and the failure mode is immediate: "server unreachable" on a server that's actually fine, just slightly slower to boot.

**Why:** `scripts/deploy.sh`'s `sleep 3` after `launchctl kickstart -k` had worked reliably for a long time. Adding `_compact_checkpoint_db()` to server startup (task:b623ea48) pushed boot time past 3s consistently, and the fixed-sleep health check started failing on every single deploy — the server was healthy, just not within 3 seconds.

**How to apply:** Prefer a poll loop (retry health check every 1s, up to some generous ceiling) over any fixed sleep before checking whether a process/service is ready — costs nothing extra when things are fast, and doesn't need re-tuning every time startup work changes. When a fixed sleep exists in a deploy/CI script, treat it as a latent bug waiting for the next person to add startup work, not a settled constant.
